attributes.stem:
You are seeing a 66-year-old man who presents to the Emergency Department with confusion and urinary symptoms. He has a medical history of metastatic pancreatic cancer diagnosed three months ago. He has recently seen his oncologist and decided on a comfort-based approach, being referred to a palliative care physician. He has been given an expected life expectancy of three to six months. He does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears uncomfortable and somewhat confused. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. He has mild suprapubic tenderness on palpation.

He has been commenced on intravenous antibiotics for a urinary tract infection. At the end of your consultation, he asks about voluntary assisted dying.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate response to his request?
options: Refer him to the voluntary assisted dying assessment process
Advise him to discuss this with his family first
Explore his reasons for the request and provide information about the process
Tell him that voluntary assisted dying is not an option for him
Assess his decision-making capacity for this request
attributes.explanation:
When a patient inquires about voluntary assisted dying (VAD), it is crucial to approach the topic with empathy, openness, and without judgement. The most appropriate response in this instance is to explore his reasons for the request and provide information about the process. This approach allows for a thorough understanding of the patient's concerns, fears, and motivations while also ensuring he has accurate information about VAD.

Voluntary assisted dying (VAD) is legal in all Australian States and will be in the ACT from Nov 2025, with each jurisdiction having its own legal framework and referral process. It remains unlawful in the Northern territory. However there are some similarities and overlap in eligibility criteria, a patient must meet ALL criteria in their jurisdiction to be eligible. The minimum basic criteria which is consistent across all states include ;

  • Patient must be over the age of 18

  • Be a resident of the state they are requesting VAD in for at least 12 months

  • Have decision making capacity for VAD

  • Are acting voluntarily and without coercion

  • Have an enduring request for VAD (i.e. their request is ongoing)

  • Have a disease, illness or medical condition that is, advanced and will cause death.

  • Disease is expected to or will cause death within six months, or 12 months if a person has a neurodegenerative condition (Victoria, Western Australia, South Australia, New South Wales, and Tasmania)

  • Causing suffering that cannot be relieved in a way that the person finds tolerable

The above is by no means an exhaustive list and each state has different eligibility criteria, but it gives a rough guideline of the common factors across the states in Australia.

Referring him to the voluntary assisted dying assessment process would be premature at this stage. Before considering VAD, it's important to ensure that all palliative care options have been fully explored and that the patient has a clear understanding of what VAD entails. Additionally, given his current confused state, there may be questions about his decision-making capacity, which is a crucial criterion for VAD eligibility. It may be appropriate to seek the assistance of a VAD navigator or referral service to discuss this case or organise review once patient has been deemed to have capacity and is making consistent request.

Advising him to discuss this with his family first, while well-intentioned, is not the most appropriate first response. While family discussions are often an important part of end-of-life decision-making, the initial conversation about VAD should be between the healthcare provider and the patient. The patient's autonomy should be respected, and he should be given the opportunity to express his thoughts and receive information before involving family members.

Telling him that voluntary assisted dying is not an option for him is not appropriate without first exploring his situation in detail. While his current confused state might raise questions about his decision-making capacity, this could be temporary due to his urinary tract infection, and he may meet all the criteria once his confusion or delirium has resolved. A thorough assessment would be needed to determine his eligibility for VAD.

Assessing his decision-making capacity for this request is an important and essential step in the VAD process, but it is not the most appropriate initial response. Before assessing capacity, it's crucial to understand the patient's motivations and provide information about the process. Capacity assessment would typically occur as part of a formal VAD assessment, if the patient decides to pursue this option after the initial discussion. This patient's current mild confusion does not necessarily prevent him from having capacity to request VAD - particularly if the confusion is in the context of a urinary tract infection.

In exploring his request, it's important to discuss his current symptoms, fears about the future, and goals of care. This conversation might reveal concerns that can be addressed through other means, such as improved symptom management or psychological support. If the patient continues to express interest in VAD after this discussion, and if it is legal in the jurisdiction, then providing information about the formal assessment process, eligibility criteria, and alternative options would be appropriate. It's also crucial to assure the patient of continued support and care, regardless of his decision about VAD.
learningPoints: Voluntary assisted dying requests require careful assessment of decision-making capacity and eligibility criteria.
Palliative care options should be fully explored before considering voluntary assisted dying.
Discussions about voluntary assisted dying should be approached with empathy, openness, and without judgement.
Voluntary assisted dying (VAD) is legal in all Australian States, with each jurisdiction having its own legal framework and referral process.
references: End of Life Directions for Aged Care (ELDAC). Overview of voluntary assisted dying. Published online 28 August 2024. Available online at ; https://www.eldac.com.au/Portals/12/Documents/Factsheet/Legal/VAD-Overview.pdf

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree

attributes.name: Level III
attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
